---
title: "Supervised Learning, Losses, and Empirical Risk"
description: "A bridge page showing how prediction tasks, loss functions, and empirical risk connect the site's math foundations to the basic language of machine learning."
page-type: application
status: published
update-status: current
level: bridge
topic: machine-learning
prerequisites:
  - /topics/foundation/probability/index.qmd
  - /topics/foundation/statistics/index.qmd
  - /topics/foundation/linear-algebra/applications/linear-regression-through-projection.qmd
learning-goals:
  - "Explain supervised learning as prediction from examples."
  - "Interpret a loss function as a numerical penalty for prediction error."
  - "Explain empirical risk as average training loss over a sample."
  - "Connect empirical risk language to optimization, validation, and generalization."
applications:
  - machine-learning
  - machine-learning-theory
keywords:
  - supervised learning
  - loss
  - empirical risk
  - generalization
  - optimization
maintainer: mtuann
last-reviewed: 2026-04-24
source-check-date: 2026-04-24
review-cadence: semiannual
date-modified: last-modified
draft: false
---

## Application Snapshot

Most of modern machine learning can be described in one compact sentence:

`choose a model, measure its prediction error with a loss, and try to make the average loss small on data`

That average loss is the empirical risk.

This page is the shortest bridge from the site's current math foundations into the language used in ML courses, papers, and benchmarks.

## Problem Setting

In supervised learning, we observe examples

$$
(x_1,y_1), \dots, (x_n,y_n),
$$

where:

- $x_i$ is an input
- $y_i$ is the target or label

We choose a predictor $f$ from some model class and measure how well it predicts $y_i$ from $x_i$.

A loss function

$$
\ell(f(x),y)
$$

assigns a numerical penalty to a prediction-target pair.

The empirical risk of $f$ on the sample is

$$
\hat{R}_n(f) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n \ell(f(x_i), y_i).
$$

The training problem is then:

$$
\min_f \hat{R}_n(f)
$$

possibly with regularization or constraints.

## Why This Math Appears

This language reuses several math layers already on the site:

- `Probability`: data are modeled as random draws from some distribution
- `Statistics`: training loss is a sample-based quantity, so estimation and validation matter
- `Linear Algebra`: many predictors are linear maps, projections, or low-rank approximations
- `Proofs + Logic`: theory papers formalize guarantees using quantified assumptions and implications

So empirical risk is not a separate “ML trick.” It is the meeting point where modeling, optimization, probability, and statistics all touch the same object.

## Math Objects In Use

- sample of input-target pairs $(x_i,y_i)$
- predictor $f$
- loss function $\ell$
- empirical risk $\hat{R}_n(f)$
- sometimes a regularized objective such as

  $$
  \hat{R}_n(f) + \lambda \Omega(f)
  $$

## A Small Worked Walkthrough

Suppose we fit a linear predictor

$$
f_\beta(x) = x^\top \beta
$$

for a regression task and use squared loss:

$$
\ell(f_\beta(x_i), y_i) = (x_i^\top \beta - y_i)^2.
$$

Then the empirical risk becomes

$$
\hat{R}_n(\beta)
=
\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n (x_i^\top \beta - y_i)^2.
$$

Up to the scaling factor $1/n$, this is the same least-squares objective you already saw in [Linear Regression Through Projection](../../topics/foundation/linear-algebra/applications/linear-regression-through-projection.qmd).

That is why least squares is such a good first ML bridge:

- it is already empirical risk minimization
- the loss is explicit
- the math object is familiar
- later models mostly change the predictor class, the loss, or the regularizer

## Implementation or Computation Note

Empirical risk is the starting object, not the end of the story.

In practice, once the objective is written down, the workflow immediately branches into three operational questions:

1. `Optimization`
   How do we actually minimize the objective?

2. `Validation`
   How do we choose models and hyperparameters without fooling ourselves?

3. `Generalization`
   Why should low sample loss say anything about future data?

That is why this page is the first bridge into the rest of the ML section rather than a self-contained stopping point.

Use these pages as the strongest follow-on support:

- [Linear Regression Through Projection](../../topics/foundation/linear-algebra/applications/linear-regression-through-projection.qmd)
- [Optimization for Machine Learning](optimization-for-machine-learning.qmd)
- [Generalization, Overfitting, and Validation](generalization-overfitting-and-validation.qmd)
- [AI / ML Theory Roadmap](../../roadmaps/ai-ml-theory.qmd)

## Failure Modes

- treating empirical risk as if it were the true population objective
- forgetting that the loss function is a modeling choice, not a law of nature
- thinking lower training loss automatically means better generalization
- confusing the model class with the optimization method used to fit it
- talking about `accuracy` when the task is actually a regression task with a different loss

## Paper Bridge

- [CS229: Machine Learning](https://cs229.stanford.edu/index.html) - `First pass` - official Stanford entry point where supervised learning and loss-based objectives are the default language from the beginning. Checked `2026-04-24`.
- [CS229T / Statistical Learning Theory](https://web.stanford.edu/class/cs229t/) - `Paper bridge` - a direct continuation once you want to see empirical risk and ERM turned into formal generalization questions. Checked `2026-04-24`.

## Sources and Further Reading

- [CS229: Machine Learning](https://cs229.stanford.edu/index.html) - `First pass` - official current ML course hub where supervised learning and loss-based training are central. Checked `2026-04-24`.
- [CS 189 Syllabus](https://eecs189.org/sp26/syllabus/) - `First pass` - official Berkeley course framing the full ML pipeline from problem setup to model design and optimization. Checked `2026-04-24`.
- [Mathematics for Machine Learning](https://mml-book.github.io/) - `Second pass` - useful bridge for readers translating familiar math objects into ML notation. Checked `2026-04-24`.
- [CS229T / Statistical Learning Theory](https://web.stanford.edu/class/cs229t/) - `Paper bridge` - points from empirical risk language toward formal learning-theory analysis. Checked `2026-04-24`.
